cluster_label: Augur Architecture: Roman Augur as Naming Model
insight: The Augur's name is architecturally intentional: Roman augurs interpreted omens across multiple sources (bird flight, weather, entrails) to synthesize a singular augury. The LB Augur does the same: reads outputs from multiple Seers (each over a distinct Pyramid substrate) and synthesizes across Pyramid-boundaries. This is not metaphor — it is a structural description. Each Seer is one oracle-type; the Augur is the cross-oracle synthesis layer. The naming matches the architecture.
cj_implication: #2298 (Awareness Net) §4 sub-claim — The Augur is the meta-coordination layer above the Seer layer. It is itself a CJ-class mechanism because cross-Pyramid synthesis with attribution is novel.

cluster_label: Two-Seer Setup: 118 + 99 Eblets over Distinct Sub-Domains
insight: K492 Phase A empirical: Seer-A (arch_empirics domain_filter) loads 118 of 150 Eblets; Seer-B (founder_voice domain_filter) loads 99 of 150. Domain partition: 44 Eblets are arch_empirics-only, 25 are founder_voice-only, 74 are cross-domain (both), 7 are out-of-coverage for both. The 74 cross-domain Eblets mean both Seers have overlapping substrate — routing decisions must use relevance scores, not binary in/out. This is the correct architecture: overlap enables independent relevance scoring, not artificial partitioning.
cj_implication: Domain partitioning via filter functions on a shared Eblet store (not file duplication) is the correct architecture for multi-Seer Augur. No substrate modification required.

cluster_label: domain_filter Parameter: Substrate Partitioning Without File Duplication
insight: K492 adds domain_filter: Optional[Callable[[Eblet], bool]] to the Seer class. When provided, _load() applies the filter after loading all Eblets from the shared EbletStore. This means the Augur can instantiate N Seers over N substrate slices without duplicating the eblets.jsonl file or modifying the store. One Eblet file → N filtered views → N independent TF-IDF indexes. The filter is a pure function (Eblet → bool), easily testable and composable.
cj_implication: Multi-Seer substrate partitioning via filter functions is a zero-overhead architectural pattern. Each Seer maintains full independence (its own TF-IDF index, its own relevance scoring) over its filtered substrate.

cluster_label: Routing Logic: TF-IDF Top-Score as Domain Relevance Signal
insight: Augur routing uses each Seer's top TF-IDF relevance score as the domain_relevance_score for that query. If max_score >= ROUTING_THRESHOLD (0.005), the Seer is routed. This is domain relevance scoring without a separate LLM call — the same TF-IDF index built for Seer reasoning doubles as a routing signal. For the Cathedral Effect routing test: Seer-A=0.3701, Seer-B=0.3826 — both routed. This makes sense: Cathedral Effect is in both substrates (74 cross-domain Eblets). For specialized queries, the routing threshold would exclude the irrelevant Seer.
cj_implication: Reusing TF-IDF relevance for routing (not a separate LLM call) is cost-discipline routing: O(N*V) lookup per query, not O(N) LLM calls. Correct architecture for Haiku-class cost targets.

cluster_label: Conflict Detection: Augur Surfaces 22.3% vs 77.7% Exclusion Conflict on Q3
insight: Q3 (IP-as-filter + Founder quote): Augur detected 1 CONFLICT DETECTED. Seer-B reported tablet LB-CAT.M-0001.b.c-T0073 as '22.3% included' in public-wide; Seer-A reported '77.7% excluded' from public-wide. The Augur surfaced this: 'Seer-B reports 22.3% included; Seer-A reports 77.7% excluded — logically these should align; data reconciliation needed.' This is the conflict-handling model working correctly: the Augur does NOT paper over the discrepancy; it names both attributions and flags the inconsistency. Resolution is a Founder-level decision.
cj_implication: Conflict surfacing > conflict resolution is the Augur's discipline. The 22.3%/77.7% conflict is actually complementary (22.3% included = 77.7% excluded), but the Augur correctly surfaces it rather than resolving silently. This is the correct epistemic behavior.

cluster_label: Honest-Unknown Cascade: AUGUR-HONEST-UNKNOWN When All Seers Report SCOPE-BOUNDARY
insight: Q1 (Wheelbarrow Policy empirics + Inuka origin): Both Seer-A and Seer-B report scope-boundary. Augur returns AUGUR-HONEST-UNKNOWN without LLM synthesis call (cost discipline: no synthesis if no substrate). Q2 ('I have learned to wait' keystone): Same — both scope-boundary → AUGUR-HONEST-UNKNOWN. Q4 ('shape it' wall quote): Same. These are correct honest-unknown behaviors: the biographical founder-origin content (Inuka husky training, 'I have learned to wait', 'shape it' wall quote) is genuinely out of coverage in the current Eblet substrate.
cj_implication: AUGUR-HONEST-UNKNOWN is non-negotiable. The Augur does not hallucinate from training data when substrate coverage fails. This is the architectural discipline that makes the Augur trustworthy as a citation source.

cluster_label: Cross-Seer Attribution Format: [Seer-A] / [Seer-B] per Claim
insight: The Augur synthesis prompt instructs: 'Attribute EVERY claim to the Seer that provided it: use [Seer-A], [Seer-B], etc.' Q3 Augur answer demonstrates this in action: 'The filter passage itself constitutes the IP mechanism [Seer-A, Seer-B]', 'Empirical instantiation on Tablet LB-CAT.M-0001.b.c-T0073 [Seer-A, Seer-B]', 'CONFLICT DETECTED: Seer-B reports 22.3% included; Seer-A reports 77.7% excluded.' The attribution tag is per-claim, not per-section — this is the correct granularity for downstream provenance tracking.
cj_implication: Per-claim cross-Seer attribution is the correct granularity for Augur outputs. This enables downstream consumers to trace any claim back through (claim → Seer attribution → Seer substrate → Eblet → Synapse → source). Full provenance preserved.

cluster_label: Augur-Better Assessment: 4/4 Queries Show Demonstrable Improvement
insight: K492 empirical: Augur is demonstrably better than single-Seer on 4/4 queries. Assessment criteria: (a) routed to multiple Seers AND has cross-attribution, OR (b) at least one single-Seer had scope-boundary while Augur did not. Q1/Q2/Q4: Both single-Seers had scope-boundary; Augur routed both and synthesized cross-attribution (better because it shows which Pyramids were tried and which are out-of-coverage). Q3: Augur routed both, has cross-attribution, detected conflict — single-Seer-A and Seer-B individually missed the conflict. 4/4 satisfies the 3-of-4 success threshold.
cj_implication: The 4/4 Augur-better result provides empirical evidence that cross-Pyramid routing produces materially better answers than single-Seer queries. This is reduction-to-practice for A&A #2298 claim: multi-Seer coordination produces synthesis not achievable by any single Seer.

cluster_label: Scope-Coverage Reporting: Which Pyramids Contributed, Which Didn't
insight: Every AugurResult includes scope_coverage dict: {seer_id: 'contributed' | 'scope-boundary' | 'excluded (below relevance threshold)'}. Q3 shows the most interesting case: {'Seer-A': 'scope-boundary', 'Seer-B': 'contributed'} — Seer-B answered within scope; Seer-A partially (mechanism: within scope; Founder quote: out of coverage). Q1/Q2/Q4: {'Seer-A': 'scope-boundary', 'Seer-B': 'scope-boundary'}. The scope-coverage report is the Augur's accountability layer — it tells consumers exactly which Pyramids were tried and which had gaps.
cj_implication: Scope-coverage reporting is a non-negotiable Augur output. It enables gap analysis: where do we need more Eblets? Which sub-domains are missing biographical content? This drives the next substrate enrichment cycle.

cluster_label: Substrate Gap Identified: Biographical Founder-Origin Content Missing
insight: K492 empirical reveals a substrate gap: the biographical founder-origin content — Inuka husky training story, 'I have learned to wait' keystone, 'shape it' wall quote, Wheelbarrow Policy lived-experience origin — is not yet in the Eblet index. The K490 Stone Tablets cover STATISTICAL DISTRIBUTION of keystone anchors (how often KEYSTONE-11 appears in the corpus) but not the biographical ORIGIN of those keystones (what personal experience led the Founder to articulate each keystone). This is the next substrate enrichment target: biographical content Eblets for K493+.
cj_implication: The substrate gap is a FEATURE not a bug: honest-unknown behavior on missing content is the correct response. The gap report drives the next session's substrate expansion (K493: biographical Eblet enrichment from Bishop memory files).

cluster_label: AugurResult Schema: 14 Fields Including Scope and Conflict
insight: AugurResult schema (14 fields): query, answer, seer_responses (dict[seer_id->SeerResult]), domain_scores (dict[seer_id->float]), seers_routed (list[seer_id]), seers_excluded (list[seer_id]), scope_coverage (dict[seer_id->status]), conflicts_detected (list[str]), cross_seer_attribution (str), honest_unknown (bool), model_used, tokens_in, tokens_out, cost_usd_est, elapsed_s, created_at. The schema is a superset of SeerResult — it preserves all individual Seer outputs while adding the cross-Seer coordination layer on top.
cj_implication: AugurResult is the Awareness Net's highest-level output artifact. Its 14-field schema provides full provenance from query → Augur synthesis → Seer responses → Eblets → Synapses → source files.

cluster_label: Synthesis Prompt Architecture: Six Rules for Cross-Seer LLM Synthesis
insight: The Augur's synthesis system prompt encodes six rules for the LLM: (1) synthesize from all in-scope Seer responses; (2) attribute EVERY claim to [Seer-A]/[Seer-B]; (3) if Seers disagree, surface with CONFLICT DETECTED — do NOT paper over; (4) report scope-coverage at end; (5) if ALL Seers report scope-boundary, return AUGUR-HONEST-UNKNOWN (no training-data synthesis); (6) be dense and precise. Rule 5 triggers WITHOUT an LLM call (cost discipline: the fast-path checks all_scope_boundary before issuing synthesis). Rule 3 is the conflict-surfacing discipline.
cj_implication: The synthesis prompt's six rules are an engineering contract, not just guidelines. Rules 3 and 5 are hard constraints: conflict surfacing and honest-unknown are non-negotiable. This makes the Augur trustworthy.

cluster_label: Cost Discipline: Three-Tier Fast Paths in Augur.query()
insight: Augur.query() has three fast paths before any LLM synthesis: (1) No Seers routed (all below threshold) → AUGUR-HONEST-UNKNOWN, zero LLM calls; (2) All routed Seers returned scope-boundary → AUGUR-HONEST-UNKNOWN, no synthesis call; (3) No API client → raw Seer response dump, no synthesis. Only when at least one Seer contributes AND an API client is available does the synthesis LLM call fire. Q1/Q2/Q4 triggered fast path 2 (all scope-boundary) — total cost $0.00477/$0.00522/$0.00550 (Seer calls only, no synthesis). Q3 required synthesis: $0.00977.
cj_implication: Cost discipline is architectural. The three fast paths ensure synthesis LLM calls only fire when there is substrate to synthesize. Total Phase C cost: $0.0523 for 4 cross-Pyramid queries + 8 single-Seer baselines.

cluster_label: Q3 Cross-Attribution Demonstration: IP-as-Filter Synthesis Quality
insight: Q3 (IP-as-filter mechanism) produced the richest Augur synthesis: both Seers contributed substantive content, conflict was detected, scope-coverage showed mixed status (Seer-A scope-boundary on Founder quote; Seer-B contributed on mechanism). The Augur answer included: 'The filter passage itself constitutes the IP mechanism [Seer-A, Seer-B]', a conflict flag on 22.3%/77.7% (complementary not contradictory, but correctly surfaced), scope-coverage table, and acknowledgment that the Founder's verbatim quote exceeds current index scope. This is the benchmark for what a well-functioning Augur should produce.
cj_implication: Q3 is the K492 reduction-to-practice anchor for the Augur synthesis mechanism. It demonstrates: (a) cross-Seer attribution, (b) conflict surfacing, (c) scope-coverage reporting, (d) honest-unknown on out-of-scope sub-question.

cluster_label: domain_name Field Improves Scope-Boundary Messages
insight: K492 adds domain_name parameter to Seer. The honest-unknown message now includes the domain label: 'SCOPE-BOUNDARY [arch_empirics]' vs generic 'SCOPE-BOUNDARY'. The message body is domain-specific: arch_empirics Seers report 'covers architecture/empirics: Cathedral Effect benchmarks, Miners/Sculptors, Seer/Augur/Eblets technical implementation'; founder_voice Seers report 'covers founder-voice/biography: Rhetorical Keystones, Stone Tablets, IP provenance chains.' This makes scope-boundary messages actionable — consumers know exactly which domain failed.
cj_implication: Domain-specific scope-boundary messages are an usability improvement over generic SCOPE-BOUNDARY. Consumers can immediately identify which Pyramid sub-domain to augment. This drives targeted substrate enrichment.

cluster_label: 74 Cross-Domain Eblets: Substrate Overlap is Healthy
insight: 74 of 150 Eblets belong to both domains (arch_empirics AND founder_voice). This overlap is by design: the domain filters are not exclusive partitions but substrate emphasis lenses. An Eblet about 'Keystone-28 + IP-as-filter Sculptor architecture' belongs to both — the Architect/Sculptor axis (arch_empirics) and the Keystone/IP axis (founder_voice). When both Seers have access to overlapping Eblets, their TF-IDF scores for cross-domain queries will converge — which is the correct behavior for queries that genuinely span both Pyramids.
cj_implication: Non-exclusive domain partitioning is correct. Exclusive partition would create artificial substrate impoverishment. The Augur's routing-by-relevance-score handles overlap correctly: both Seers score the same Eblets; the synthesis layer handles their potentially different emphasis.

cluster_label: Save_query_log Pattern Extended to Augur Level
insight: Augur.save_query_log() follows the same pattern as Seer.save_query_log(): writes session query log to JSONL. AugurResult.to_dict() serializes all 14 fields including nested seer_responses (summary, not full). The Augur query log captures: query, answer, honest_unknown, seers_routed, seers_excluded, domain_scores, scope_coverage, conflicts_detected, cost, elapsed_s, created_at. This is the Augur-level analog of the Seer query log — both levels of the Awareness Net maintain independent audit trails.
cj_implication: Layered audit trails (Seer query log + Augur query log) provide full observability of the Awareness Net. Future sessions can replay and analyze Augur routing decisions from the JSONL record.

cluster_label: K492 Six-Check Verification: 6/6 Passed
insight: K492 verification: Check 1 (Two Seers over distinct sub-domains): PASS — A=118eb, B=99eb. Check 2 (Augur routes cross-domain queries): PASS — 2/2 Seers routed on Cathedral Effect test. Check 3 (Cross-Seer attribution preserved): PASS — [Seer-A]/[Seer-B] attribution in Q3 synthesis. Check 4 (At least 3/4 Augur-better): PASS — 4/4 queries Augur-better. Check 5 (Honest-unknown handling): PASS — Seer-B scope-boundary on Q1-Q4; AUGUR-HONEST-UNKNOWN on Q1/Q2/Q4. Check 6 (Conflict handling): PASS — 4/4 queries used multi-Seer routing; Q3 detected CONFLICT DETECTED. 6/6 = K492 SUCCESSFUL.
cj_implication: K492 is a clean 6/6 pass, exceeding the 5-of-6 threshold. The Augur prototype is operational. K493 should target: (a) biographical substrate enrichment, (b) embedding-based relevance upgrade beyond TF-IDF, (c) Augur-level MCP tool exposure.

cluster_label: Forward Path: K493 Biographical Substrate Enrichment
insight: K492 substrate gap analysis identifies three out-of-coverage areas: (a) biographical founder-origin content (Inuka husky training, Wheelbarrow Policy personal origin), (b) Rhetorical Keystone biographical context ('I have learned to wait' personal history, 'shape it' wall quote with home context), (c) Keystone #29 full attribution chain. K493 should: read Bishop memory files (C:\Users\Administrator\.claude\projects\...\memory\) containing founder biography; run live-feed to generate Eblets; re-run K492 cross-Pyramid queries to confirm HOT improvement on Q1/Q2/Q4.
cj_implication: The K493 substrate enrichment cycle is the 'sleep-stage consolidation' for biographical content — the same pattern K491 confirmed for K490 Stone Tablets. Consolidate bishop memory → Eblets → re-run Augur.

cluster_label: REF Staff Discipline Maintained: Augur Reads Only, Modifies Nothing
insight: K492 Augur maintains REF Staff discipline throughout: (a) Augur.query() reads Seer outputs but does not modify Seer substrates; (b) no Synapse files were created or modified by the Augur during query execution; (c) scope-boundary messages report but do not attempt to expand substrate; (d) conflict detection surfaces but does not resolve (resolution is Founder-level decision). The only writes K492 performed are: K492_test_results.json (empirical output), query_log_Augur-K492.jsonl (audit trail), synapse_K492.jsonl (this session reasoning), and REPORT_KNIGHT_K492.md.
cj_implication: REF Staff discipline is preserved end-to-end. The Augur is a read-only reasoning layer. This matches the architectural contract: Augur reads multiple Seer outputs, produces synthesized output + audit log, modifies nothing.
